_source: Legend in the Mist — Action Grimoire (supplement). Verbatim via NotebookLM, extracted section by section. PARTIAL: filled in as sections are added. Merged into LITM_DATA.actionGrimoire by inject.py.

sections.intro:
Nearly everyone in a medieval fantasy setting
has handicraft skills and access to simple
tools. Many Heroes enter the story as an
apprentice artisan of a particular craft, and
nearly all carry implements, weapons, or armor that
must be maintained to function properly and that could
be improved by a master artisan.

Common Crafting
Tags & Statuses

BREAKING EQUIPMENT
Transports such as carts and boats and complex
machines, such as siege weapons and the mechanism
of a windmill have a destroy Limit, on which they
take statuses representing structural damage (such as
damaged) that prevents them from being operated as
intended. Maxing out this Limit means the object has
been damaged beyond the point of operation.
Similarly, obstructions such as locked doors or
barricades have destroy Limits that must be maxed
before they can be easily bypassed (unless you
manage to pick the lock, see Burglary on page 38).

DISREPAIR
Repeated use, travel, and deliberate sabotage can all
affect gear such as weapons and armor, leaving them
with a loud squeaking, loose hilt, or leaning slightly
to the left. If damage to a piece of gear prevents the
use of it altogether, such as a broken bow string or a
sword without a handle, scratch its tag instead, and
re-create it once the gear is fixed. See Maintaining
and Repairing Equipment (page 13).

SUPPLY ISSUES
The fuel, food, or ammunition supply of a Hero,
the Fellowship, a campsite or shelter, a community,
pack animal, transport, machine, or weapon is
usually handled narratively. The Narrator can make
Threats of supply running low ("You've burned
half your wood by now") and use Consequences to
announce supplies have run out, which prohibits
any further actions using that item ("You're out of
arrows.", Blocked). You can take action or spend
Power on an extra feat in a related action (such as
while foraging in the forest) to regain supply and
remove the narrative restriction. You can also prevent
a supply Consequence for an action by using an extra
feat, representing using supply economically.
Supply issues can also be tracked in a Detailed way,
with a status. This is recommended for traveling
groups as a whole. For example, the Fellowship
might have food and munitions issues and be
running short-on-supplies, affecting their camp
actions. Removing this status requires resupplying,
see Making a Purchase (page 72) and Living off
the Land (page 44).
Tags representing supplies can:
• Inspire the Narrator to use supply-related Threats
& Consequences more or less often
• Affect resupply actions
Affect actions that require ample supply to
perform. For example, extra coal can help when
continuously loading a blacksmith's furnace,
and a feed bag with a hole could negatively affect
prolonged ride.

entries.name: Crafting and Tinkering
entries.explanation:
You use your trained crafting abilities to create a useful piece of equipment: a weapon, a relic, a potion, a transport, etc. Alternatively, you work on an existing piece of gear to make it perform better than before. The action could reflect a quick, tinkered assembly while at camp using materials and tools at hand or a days-long project at your workshop.
For complex or time-consuming projects, the Narrator can decide to set a complete Limit. When maxed out, you gain a story tag or a theme, as decided by the Narrator.
Consider Might when working on an item that would be larger than a person or affect more than one person, or on a magical artifact, or to craft or improve many items.
This action is often taken while camping or sojourning. See also the Crafting Journey, Corebook Vol. II, page 194.
See also Crafting Magical Items (page 65). Tο sell your crafted items for coin, see Selling Goods (page 74).

positive: appropriate crafting skills (e.g., weapon smithing, whittling, golem building)
mentorship or direction from a master of the craft
negative: complexity of the recipe, floorplan, or diagram

entries.success:
CREATE a story tag for the item, or a theme representing the item and its abilities, such as engraved elm bow, makeshift lockpicks, hidden compartment, or bronze cuirass. If you create a theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
BESTOW existing equipment with tags representing new features, like adding honed edge or intimidating runes to your family sword, or wax sealing your boots to make them waterproof. If this creates a theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
ADVANCE an ongoing project with making-progress.
extraFeats: Adjust the item's size or shape.
Do it quickly or in secret.
Conceal your alterations (the item does not appear to be changed).
Use fewer materials.
Craft an item of superior quality (the Narrator cannot add a negative story tag).
Make it in the style of a particular crafting guild or culture, or add your signature.
consequences: >> You waste time, break tools, or expend resources (scratch tags or short-on-coin).
>> The item is faulty or flawed (add negative story tag).
>> You have a work accident (scorched, wounded, bruised, cramps, painful hand, etc.).
>>> Your work attracts unwanted attention (Exposure).
>>> You activate a dangerous functionality in the artifact (Ill Tidings).
>> You overwork yourself or your apprentices (exhausted, resentful, or scratch a tag).
entries.might: Consider Might when working on an item that would be larger than a person or affect more than one person, or on a magical artifact, or to craft or improve many items.

entries.name: Farming and Mining
actionExamples: Prospect a river rich in ore
entries.explanation:
You build or operate an endeavor with the intention to harvest some commodity such as cotton, silver, or produce. This could be a specific day-to-day action, one of many needed for the upkeep of the operation, or an entire season of production captured in a single roll, especially if you are sojourning.
To process your bounty into useful items, see Crafting and Tinkering (page 11). To trade it for coin, see Selling Goods (page 74). For cooking hearty meals with your bounty, see Invigoration (page 51). If your farm involves livestock, see also Animal Kinship and Handling (page 43).
Consider Might if you are farming or mining on a large scale or in extreme conditions.

positive: guidance from a manual or a mentor
negative: scarcity of needed resources such as water and nutrients

entries.success:
QUICK You've completed the task at hand so production can continue.
CREATE a story tag for the produce or commodity you have generated such as a pail of milk, gold ore, or bushel of silk bulbs.
extraFeats: Finish the work with some time to spare (keep the spotlight).
Avoid exhausting yourself or your laborers.
Avoid breaking tools or wasting supplies.
Complete your work in peace, without anyone bothering you.
Produce something neat as a by-product.
Gain the appreciation of your community or neighbors.
consequences: >> You waste time, break tools, or expend resources (scratch tags or short-on-coin).
>> The commodity is of poor quality or less than expected (add negative story tag).
>>> You have a work accident (wounded, bruised, cramps, painful hand, etc.).
>>> Someone covets your farm or mine (Exposure).
>>> A new problem crops up and threatens your work (Complication).
>>> Your farm or mine create problems for nearby dwellers (New Challenge).
>>> You overwork yourself or your laborers (exhausted, resentful, or scratch a tag).
entries.might: Consider Might if you are farming or mining on a large scale or in extreme conditions.

entries.name: Harvesting Ingredients
actionExamples: Find a specific herb, mushroom, moss, etc..
Dissect a monster to obtain a specific organ, gland, or humor
Gather faerie dust on Midsummer's Eve
Pick a mind for a specific memory
Loot a boobytrapped chest, or body
entries.explanation:
You obtain a rare ingredient or component through a skillful process of location, identification, and collection.
Consider Might if the process requires extraordinary knowledge or ability.

positive: appropriate skill such as foraging, chirurgy, or animal husbandry
appropriate lore such as botany or monster lore
the target of harvesting (glade, carcass, etc.)
negative: natural or unnatural defenses of the target

entries.success:
QUICK You successfully obtain the ingredient.
CREATE a story tag for the ingredient you collected such as a Rime Thistle extract, cockatrice venom, or a beating heart.
extraFeats: Leave the target undisturbed and without a trace.
Harvest discreetly (no one knows you harvested).
Extract an ingredient of very fine quality.
Avoid harm to yourself or your tools while harvesting.
If possible, allow further harvesting from the same source.
consequences: >> You waste time or break tools (time-passes or scratch tags).
>>> You despoil or lose the source of the ingredient (Blocked and scratch tags).
>> The ingredient is of poor quality or little quantity (add negative story tag).
>> You have a work accident (wounded, bruised, cramps, painful hand, etc.).
>>> Someone covets your ingredient or its source (Exposure).
>> You disturb a local in the process of extraction (New Challenge).
entries.might: Consider Might if the process requires extraordinary knowledge or ability.

entries.name: Maintaining and Repairing Equipment
entries.explanation:
You use your crafting skills to keep your equipment functioning by repairing, upkeeping, or replacing broken parts.
This action covers serious repairs, of the sort that demand taking special care, and therefore a roll. This action is often taken while camping or sojourning.

positive: having a relationship with the item
negative: defect that is subtle or complex
heavy damage or wear and tear.
dirt, muck, or sand in the parts
The removed status or tag does not count as a negative for this action, unless it is especially egregious.

entries.success: RESTORE by removing negative statuses or tags and recover scratched tags of a weapon, piece of gear, transport, or artifact.
extraFeats: Do it quietly.
The repaired damage leaves no mark or trace.
Keep the spotlight to take immediate action with the repaired item.
consequences: >>> You make things worse (inflict a different status or tag, or scratch a tag).
>>> Your tools break or you use up all your extra parts (short-on-supplies or scratch a tag).
>>> You misplace the item in question (Complication).
>>> You become too engrossed in the work (distracted).

entries.name: Sabotaging
entries.explanation:
You disrupt the operation of a machine, trap, transport, or riding animal using your crafting skills, careful method, or brute strength. You might be trying to stop a specific function, or just get the thing to stop working.
Consider Might if the device is bigger than a person, if it relies on magic or design you do not understand, or if you're targeting a specific part in it.
If you're trying to get through a door or other passage, see also Burglary (page 38).

negative: item is of high quality craftsmanship

entries.success:
QUICK The item ceases to operate, or malfunctions in the way you intended.
DISRUPT by giving negative statuses such as damaged or stuttering. If the item is big, the Narrator might decide to give it a destroy Limit.
WEAKEN by removing tags from the item representing its functions, or protective statuses such as fortified.
RESTORE by removing devices that afflict you such as restrained, or restricted magic.
extraFeats: Do it quietly.
Remove any traces of tampering.
Delay the effect so that it is triggered later.
Keep the spotlight for a follow-up action with the item.
The damage lasts longer or is hard to repair.
consequences: >>> Your tools break (scratch a tag).
>> You have a work accident (scorched, wounded, bruised, cramps, painful hand, etc.).
>>> There are unintended victims (Ill Tidings).
>>> You are discovered, or your activity is noticed (suspicious, alarm, or Exposure).
>> You damage a related or nearby functionality you did not intend to (Complication).
>> You become too engrossed in the work (distracted).
entries.might: Consider Might if the device is bigger than a person, if it relies on magic or design you do not understand, or if you're targeting a specific part in it.

entries.name: Area Attacks
actionExamples: Shower with a hail of arrows.
Infuse an area with evil spirits
Blanket area with magical hoar frost
entries.explanation:
You deliver an attack to an entire area. Targets in the area are hit selectively or indiscriminately, depending on the nature of your attack. If there are defensive tags shared by most of the targets, they apply to this roll; otherwise, targets defend from this attack individually (Area Effect Actions, Corebook Vol. I, page 178).
This action often requires some Might to perform, depending on the area covered or number of targets; see Hand-to-Hand Attacks and Ranged Attacks for attacking single targets.
entries.success: ATTACK to inflict a harmful status such as wounded, bleeding, burned, electrocuted, or corrupted, usually applied to the Limit harm or destroy.
extraFeats: Prevent a counterattack.
Create a mess or cause chaos.
Disperse the targets around.
Remove obstacles or hiding places.
Block the opponents from reaching a destination.
Cause the target(s) to retreat in a certain direction.
consequences: >>> You are caught in the area of effect and take the same status.
>> The targets counterattack (wounded).
>> You expose your position (Exposure).
>> You harm allies, innocents, or something valuable to you (Ill Tidings).
>>> You overexert your resources (scratch a tag).
>>> Your attack obscures the area (e.g. smoke cloud) or creates an unintended hazard (e.g. dark magic residue).
entries.might: This action often requires some Might to perform, depending on the area covered or number of targets; see Hand-to-Hand Attacks and Ranged Attacks for attacking single targets.

entries.name: Multiple Attacks
entries.explanation:
Your ability to attack multiple times in quick succession should be represented by a tag such as double strike or many-armed.
If you attack in a fashion that utilizes your quick rate of attack, add these tags to the Power of Hand-to-Hand Attacks (page 18) or Ranged Attacks (page 18). If you're attacking multiple opponents, tags for multiple attacks can help to offset the Power loss from Might. You may also be able to use multiple attack tags to unleash Area Attacks (page 17).
You can also use an extra feat to keep the spotlight for another action after your attack, but typically the Narrator will allow only one such "follow-up" attack.

entries.name: Precision Attacks
entries.explanation:
If you want to make an attack more precise to maximize damage, see Preparing an Attack (page 27) before making an Hand-to-Hand Attack (page 18) or Ranged Attack (page 18).
If you make a precise attack to impair a target's specific ability or their equipment, see Impairing Attacks (page 23) or Disarming and Sundering Gear (page 20).
If you make a precise attack to perform a stunt, see Trick Shots (page 24).

entries.name: Hand-to-Hand Attacks
entries.explanation: You strike at close range in order to harm.
entries.success: ATTACK to inflict a harmful status such as wounded, slashed, impaled, bleeding, burned, electrocuted, poisoned, or corrupted, usually applied to the Limit harm or destroy.
extraFeats: Keep the spotlight to make a quick second attack.
Prevent counterattack.
Push them back.
Block their path.
Keep them focused on you.
Draw someone else's attention.
Draw an item out of a pouch.
Maneuver around or away from them.
Keep your touch unnoticed.
consequences: >> The target counterattacks (wounded).
>>> You get off-balanced or expose yourself.
>> You are forced into an inferior-position or become blindsided.
>> You exert yourself (winded).
>>> You lose hold of your weapon (scratch a tag).
>> Your weapon gets a dull blade, your shield is worn-down, or your armor gains broken straps.

entries.name: Ranged Attacks
entries.explanation:
You shoot, throw, launch, or otherwise propel a harmful projectile, object, energy, or substance at range.
To attack an area, see Area Attacks (page 17).

positive: ideal conditions for a clear shot
negative: target's armor, cover, shielding, or dexterity

entries.success: ATTACK to inflict a harmful status such as wounded, slashed, impaled, bleeding, burned, electrocuted, poisoned, or corrupted, usually applied to the Limit harm or destroy.
extraFeats: Keep the spotlight to shoot again.
Prevent counterattack.
Keep them pinned down.
Keep them focused on you.
Create a distraction.
Stay hidden.
Misdirect the source of the attack.
Move into position for the next shot.
Slide down a hillside while firing.
consequences: >>> You lose sight of your target (Complication).
>> The target closes in, to within melee range (Complication).
>>> The target fires arrows or spells back at you (wounded).
>> You expose your position (Exposure).
>>> You are forced into an inferior-position or become blindsided.
>> You hit an unintended target (Ill Tidings).
>>>> You lose your aim.
>>> Your bow string breaks, you run out of arrows, or your magical power is exhausted (scratch a tag).

entries.name: Restraining an Opponent
entries.explanation: You use your body or weapons to limit your target's movement in close quarters. See also Hand-to-Hand Attacks (page 18).

positive: tool or weapon such as a garrote, net, or whip
negative: target is electrically charged, cold to the touch, or covered in spikes
target has many limbs, long neck, or other advantageous body shape

entries.success:
ATTACK with subdued, restrained, or pinned to max out your target's subdue Limit.
DISRUPT your target with a hindering status such as prone, concussed, pained.
extraFeats: Move them around.
Avoid their counterattack.
Position them in between you and a threat.
Cause them to drop something.
Take something off of their person.
consequences: >> The target attacks back (wounded or restrained).
>> You are thrown off-balanced or prone.
>>> You become exposed.
>>> You are forced back into an inferior-position or become blindsided.
>> You exert yourself (winded).
>> They take something from you (scratch a tag).

entries.name: Surprise Attacks
entries.explanation:
A combatant that is taken by surprise may receive the status surprised, whether through a player action, as a Consequence, or by the Narrator simply translating the narrative into game Effects.
When a Hero is surprised, the status negatively affects their reactions to what surprised them. A Hero may avoid or lessen being surprised with a reaction using tags of vigilance or advance warning.
When a Hero surprises their foes, and especially against alert opponents, the Narrator might require a player to first take an action to gain the element of surprise (give surprised). See Sneaking and Hiding (page 40).
The Narrator may also rule that certain defensive tags are inapplicable when a combatant is surprised, such as agile, a tower shield, and even certain forms of armor that an unseen attacker can easily circumvent.
To keep an attack undetected to parties other than the target, use an extra feat.

entries.name: Two-Weapon Fighting
entries.explanation: Two-weapon fighting is represented either by having two separate weapon tags, by having a tag that outlines your skill with two-weapon fighting, or both. You can perform any battle action using these tags.

entries.name: Combo Attacks
actionExamples: You can shoot your great bow at a chandelier above a target so it'll fall on them as an Attack to wound them, while also Creating a flaming chandelier story tag blocking the entrance.
You can use your vampiric magic tag suck life force to Attack and give the enemy drained-life, Weaken their energized status, and Recover to heal your own wounded status.
Your vial of acid could Weaken an enemy's heavy armor and Attack to give them acid-burns.
You can pounce from the darkness and stab a monster's limb with a silver dagger to Disrupt them with a weak limb and Influence them to be frightened!
entries.explanation: The actions in this section describe distinct battle actions, but every offensive action can potentially combine multiple Effects, depending on the tags used and the amount of Power at your disposal.

entries.name: Disarming and Sundering Gear
entries.explanation: You attack a piece of equipment on somebody's person, either held, stowed away, or attached to their body, with the goal of making it unusable or unavailable to them. Retrieving their gear might be as easy for your target as reaching over to pick a pitchfork up from the ground, or as complicated as spending weeks in a sanctum of sorcery to repair their broken magical talisman. If it is possible within the scope of a scene, the Narrator can use Consequences later in the scene to determine that the target retrieved their gear.

positive: entangling equipment or weapons such as chains, whips, or flails
negative: opponent's strong grip or extra arms
gear belts, fastenings, chains, and such
gear that is small, hard to reach, or well protected
weapons or armor hidden under cloak

entries.success: QUICK Remove an item not represented by a tag. Consequences and activities involving the item are not available until it is retrieved. WEAKEN to remove an existing tag representing gear. If you succeed, you could then Create for yourself the same tag, meaning you managed to grab hold of it and can now use it yourself (see Improvising Weapons, page 26).
extraFeats: Kick the disarmed item away or otherwise prevent it from being easily retrieved.
Keep the spotlight to immediately attack.
Prevent counterattack.
Maneuver around your opponent.
Place or plant something on your opponent.
consequences: >>> The target attacks you (wounded).
>>> You yourself are disarmed (scratch a tag) or your grip is loosened (unbalanced).
>>> The target grapples you (pinned).
>> The target secures their equipment (strong-grip; only if there was no Success).

entries.name: Feinting and Distracting Attacks
entries.explanation: You use clever tactics or manipulation such as faking an attack, enacting a false retreat, pretending to let your guard down, or creating an illusion or a loud noise in order to impair your target's judgment or cognitive capacity, forcing them to make poor choices or exposing them to a follow-up attack.

negative: target's alertness, resolve, training, sharp senses, or leadership
impaired communication, when leading a group

entries.success: QUICK The target takes the desired action. INFLUENCE to dictate the target's next actions by giving them a compelling status such as hostile to their own allies, focused on something else, docile, or overconfident. DISRUPT to inflict a hindering status such as vulnerable, overextended, guard-down, or unaware, surprised, dazed, or distracted. WEAKEN to reduce a positive tag on the target that represents preparation or commitment such as high-morale, energized, or drilled maneuver.
extraFeats: Keep the spotlight to immediately attack.
Prevent counterattack.
Draw attention away from your allies.
Move the target around the battlefield.
Split up the target group.
consequences: >>> The target attacks you while you are feinting (wounded).
>>> You get off-balanced or expose yourself.
>>> An ally gets confused as well.
>> The target sees through your misdirection and it backfires (exposed; only if there was no Success).
>>> The target wises up and makes you embarrassed or taunted (only if there was no Success).
>>> The target becomes resistant to future misdirection (alert or Blocked).

entries.name: Holding Off or Spurring Opponents
actionExamples: Repel with sword or spear maneuvers
entries.explanation: You use force or the threat of force to limit a target's tactical options, forcing them to reconsider, give up, or take certain actions, such as stepping into a specific area, revealing themselves, staying put, attacking, drawing their weapon, or activating a magical artifact. If the target does not conform to your threat, the Narrator should grant you the spotlight to Attack.
entries.success: QUICK The opponent's behavior is affected as planned. DISRUPT or INFLUENCE to give the target suppressed, cornered, threatened, demoralized, harried, or harassed.
extraFeats: Keep the spotlight to immediately attack.
Prevent counterattack.
Push them back.
Block their path.
Keep them focused on you.
Improve your position.
Force a retreat.
consequences: >>> You harm allies, innocents, or something valuable to you (Ill Tidings).
>>> You run out of ammo or magical power (scratch a tag).
>>> You get off-balanced or expose yourself.
>> You focus too much on the action (distracted).
>>> The target manages to partially circumvent your attack (e.g., only one person escapes).
>> They come to understand your tactics and avoid them (Blocked).

entries.name: Impairing Attacks
entries.explanation: You shoot or strike your target with the intention to impair a specific ability. See also Feinting and Distracting Attacks (page 21).

positive: knowledge of physiology or target's makeup

entries.success: DISRUPT by hitting a specific limb or organ, such as with a crippling shot (hobbling), kick to the head (dazed), or a strike to the ears (deafened or off-balanced). WEAKEN to remove specific ability or advantage tags, such as by shooting off a lucky hat, punching a proboscis to impair acute smell, or throwing a knife to slow down a fast runner.
extraFeats: Keep the spotlight to immediately attack.
Prevent counterattack.
Make the impairment go unnoticed.
Cause your target to flinch or be distracted.
consequences: >> The target attacks (wounded).
>>> The target is impaired, but in a different way than expected.
>>> You hit someone or something you did not intend to harm (Ill Tidings).
>>> The blow leaves your weapon with a loose hilt.
>> You run out of arrows (Blocked or scratch a tag).
>> You expose yourself to attacks (exposed).

entries.name: Provoking
actionExamples: Egg them to attack you (or someone else).
entries.explanation: You manipulate your opponent into attacking you (or someone else, although that is much harder to achieve). Consider Might if your opponent is unlikely to give you any attention or if you clearly outmatch them. To disrupt an opponent's focus, see Feinting and Distracting Attacks (page 21).

positive: opponent's fragile emotional state or propensity for rage

entries.success: QUICK The opponent will attack only you (until circumstance or Consequences change this). INFLUENCE your opponent by making them provoked or taunted, a compelling status directing them to attack you. It does not improve their attacks. DISRUPT your opponent by making them distracted, seeing-red, humiliated, etc.
extraFeats: Keep the spotlight to immediately attack, move, or parry.
Prevent or block their counterattack.
Make your provocation underhanded, unseen by anyone else.
Elegantly provoke them without sparking fury (prevent enraged).
consequences: >>> The target attacks (wounded).
>>> The target becomes enraged, which makes it harder to lessen their attacks.
>> You provoke someone or something you did not intend to (New Challenge or enraged).
>> Your motivation is plain to see (Exposure).

entries.name: Trick Shots
actionExamples: Shoot an apple off someone's head.
entries.explanation: You skillfully perform an impressive, accurate, or complicated stunt with your weapon. For example, trying to shoot and split an arrow you've already stuck into a bullseye, throwing a handaxe to cut a rope across the room, or slicing an apple in half with a sword and having each half ricochet off a different opponent's face. To harm multiple targets with a trick shot, see Ranged Attacks (page 18). To impair a target's specific ability or disarm them, use Impairing Attacks (page 23), Feinting and Distracting Attacks (page 21), or Disarming and Sundering Gear (page 20). To perform a trick shot to create a new hazard, see also Creating and Removing Hazards and Obstacles (page 48).

positive: arrows or bolts suited for the task
useful objects in the vicinity such as mirrors, hornet's nest, or weak hinges

entries.success: QUICK You successfully pull off your stunt. CREATE tags that represent new elements in the environment produced by your trick shot, such as a fallen tree, sudden flood, or flurry of falling leaves. INFLUENCE onlookers with a status such as impressed, amused, or threatened.
extraFeats: Destroy an object or remove it from the scene.
Cause others to flinch or be distracted.
Create a breach or opening.
Have the item arrive in someone's hands.
consequences: >>> You hit, but cause a reversed or undesired outcome (only if there was no Success).
>> You're embarrassed (only if there was no Success).
>> You harm someone or something you did not intend to harm (Ill Tidings).
>>> Your weapon loses its edge or aim (dull blade).

sections.intro:
Always-Relevant Tags and Statuses
Almost every action requires you to pay attention to what you're doing and be able to perceive your surroundings. For this reason, tags and statuses like darkness, heavy fog, loud crowd, distracted, pained, etc. can negatively affect most actions. Likewise, stamina statuses like tired, winded, or exhausted or mental statuses like light-headed or distressed can affect any action that requires effort or focus.

Camp & Sojourn Actions
The following repeats the list of suggested camp actions (Camping & Sojourns, Corebook Vol. I, page 179), referring to their Action Grimoire counterparts.

Time Matters
Different actions take different amounts of time to perform: a swordstroke is quick, while climbing a hill can take minutes if not hours.
As the Heroes take actions in a scene, time marches ever forward, and its passing can trigger events in the Heroes' lives. For example, searching the site of an old temple could take a good while, and as the Heroes are searching, the devils from the basements below may notice their presence, or the rogue sorcerer who searches the same ruin for an ancient relic could get closer to completing their nefarious goal.
When Establishing or re-Establishing a scene, the Narrator can always make Threats related to the passage of time, such as, "You've spent a lot of time climbing the side of the mountain; the sun is going down ominously." Later, they can deliver Consequences such as time-passes on a progress Limit (Corebook Vol. I, page 169) such as coming-storm, enchantment-fading, or speak-now-or-forever-hold-your-peace.
The Narrator can also explain the introduction of a Complication as arising due to an action taking longer than intended. For example, a Hero who is conducting a forbidden ritual at a graveyard could find themselves blackmailed by a stray vagabond who's noticed their unusual project and wants something in return for their silence.
An action's manner of performance can also affect its timing: an exceptionally well-performed negotiation could be completed quicker than usual, while a botched negotiation could burn precious daylight, often for naught. The Narrator can allow the use of an extra feat to guarantee a speedy execution.

items: Bandage or heal: Mental and Physical Resilience and Recovery (page 52), Treating Injuries and Other Conditions (page 53)
Converse: Helping and Supporting (page 86), Fellowship Preparations (page 87)
Cook: Invigoration (page 51)
Craft & repair: Crafting and Tinkering (page 11), Maintaining and Repairing Equipment (page 13)
Entertain: Social Gatherings (page 79)
Hunt & forage: Harvesting Ingredients (page 12), Living off the Land (page 44)
Prepare: Fellowship Preparations (page 87), Preparing an Approach (page 85), Withstanding and Preparing for Environmental Conditions (page 50)
Read and research: Researching a Topic (page 36)
Scout ahead or practice divination: Scouting and Guarding (page 49)
Stand watch: Scouting and Guarding (page 49)

sections.intro: The following is a list of general-use Consequences for each category of actions presented in the book, summed up here for ease of access.

lists.heading: Battle Consequences
items: An enemy lands a blow or retaliates (wounded).
An enemy gets a chance to prepare or set something up (gaining a status or tag).
An enemy escapes and closes the way behind them (Blocked).
You are disarmed, or a piece of gear is damaged or lost (worn, chipped, mucked-up, or scratch a tag).
You fall prone, are knocked-back, or stumble and become off-balanced.
You are distracted or surprised.
You are forced into an inconvenient or dangerous position (exposed, surrounded, cornered).
You become angry, panicked, or otherwise emotional.
You lose your aim, your preparedness, or your cool (remove a beneficial status).
You run out of arrows, pebbles, or other ammunition (Blocked).
You get in the way of allies (in-the-way or remove beneficial status).
You harm allies, innocents, or something valuable (wounded or scratch a tag).
The battle draws unwanted attention (Exposure), or a new enemy is already arriving (New Challenge).
Something beautiful or important is damaged or destroyed in the fight (Ill Tidings).
Your mount or vehicle gets unbalanced.
Your foe or misstep forces you into something that restrains you.

lists.heading: Magic Consequences
items: Your soul is sapped, your powers wane, or you exhaust your magical resources such as ingredients (drained, short-on-ingredients, or scratch a tag).
You or someone else suffers dangerous side-effects (wounded, cursed).
You disrupt helpful magic effects on yourself, your target, or nearby (remove a beneficial status or scratch a tag).
Your magic powers run rampant (III Tidings).
Your magic proves ineffective at this task (Blocked).
Your magic is very noticeable (Exposure).
You leave a magical scar in the area (cursed or story tag).

lists.heading: Social Consequences
items: You become embarrassed, frustrated, scared, angered, sad, disheartened, remorseful, heartbroken, or otherwise emotionally compromised.
You are convinced, charmed, tempted, or otherwise have your mind changed by someone else.
You end up paying more than you can afford (indebted).
You end up drinking more than you can handle (intoxicated).
The target changes their mind (reduce convinced).
The target demands something in return (scratch a tag), swearing an oath (sworn), or the completion of a task.
They grow suspicious, wary, or disinterested in you, making further engagements difficult.
You insult them or otherwise make them decide to cut ties with you, at least for now (Blocked).
You harm your reputation in town (disliked).
An enemy learns about your actions, or you reveal more than you intended (Exposure).
The target behaves in an unexpected way or reveals an unexpected advantage (add story tag).

lists.heading: Travel Consequences
items: You grow exhausted.
The long hours on the road take their toll and you become homesick, agitated, road-weary, or otherwise emotional.
Your equipment gets damaged or lost (worn, chipped, mucked-up, or scratch a tag).
You go through a thicket, climb over sharp rocks, or otherwise become bruised or filthy.
You are hurt by a hazard (wounded, burnt, poisoned, etc.).
The weather conditions are harsh (sunburned or frostbite).
You cross a muddy pool or clouds of insects (sickened).
You push your mount or transport too far (exhausted or damaged).
You become lost or are forced to backtrack (lost, or remove traverse).
You run out of food or water (short-on-supplies, hungry, thirsty, or scratch tags).
You are forced to take a longer route than you'd planned (time-passes).
You maneuver through an open area (exposed).
You run into a hazard or a barrier (New Challenge).
A path is no longer available: a bridge was raised, a ship is gone, the trail was covered by mudslide, etc. (Blocked).
Your activity raises suspicion (Exposure or increase the alarm or alert level of enemies).
You are discovered by a wild animal, monster, brigands, or a patrol (New Challenge).

lists.heading: Other Consequences
items: You are discovered, or your activity is noticed (suspicious, alarm, or Exposure).
You trigger a trap (wounded, poisoned, alarm, etc. or Blocked, Exposure, or New Challenge).
You run into a barrier (add story tags, Blocked, or a New Challenge).
The enemy starts keeping an eye on you (marked).
A plan of action or prepared item proves to be no longer relevant (scratch a tag).
The information you get is vague or part-true, part-false (Yes, but...).
